title: Deep Vein Thrombosis
oneLiner: A blood clot in a deep vein (usually leg), driven by stasis, hypercoagulability, or endothelial injury; it matters because it can embolise to cause pulmonary embolism and chronic venous damage.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 62-year-old man, 4 days post elective total hip replacement, develops unilateral right calf pain and swelling. The leg feels warm, the skin is mildly erythematous, and there is pitting oedema from the mid-calf down. He has been mobilising less than usual and has a history of hypertension but no prior clotting disorders. His calf circumference is 3 cm greater on the right than the left.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Postoperative (hip replacement) | **Provoked DVT**: immobility + tissue trauma = endothelial injury + stasis |
| Unilateral leg swelling | **Venous obstruction**: clot blocks outflow, increased hydrostatic pressure pushes fluid out |
| Warmth and erythema | **Local inflammatory response**: perivenous inflammation releases cytokines, dilates vessels |
| Pitting oedema | **Interstitial fluid accumulation** due to raised venous pressure |
| Asymmetric calf circumference (>3 cm) | **Highly suggestive of DVT**; even a 1–2 cm difference deserves investigation |
| Decreased mobility | **Venous stasis** → clot propagation risk |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Isolated calf DVT**: mild pain or no swelling, normal calf circumference; often missed until it propagates proximally.
- **Bilateral DVT**: rare; think malignancy (causes hypercoagulable state) or inferior vena cava filter/obstruction.
- **Upper extremity DVT** (effort thrombosis / Paget-Schroetter syndrome): arm swelling, cyanosis in a young athlete after strenuous use; also consider central line or pacemaker lead.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Definition:** Deep vein thrombosis is the formation of a blood clot (thrombus) within a deep vein, most commonly in the leg (proximal – popliteal, femoral, iliac; or distal – calf veins). The clot obstructs venous return and triggers a local inflammatory response.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency**: 1–2 per 1000 adults annually; much higher in hospitalized, surgical, or cancer patients.
- **Stakes**: 
  - Pulmonary embolism (PE) – sudden death in severe cases.
  - Post-thrombotic syndrome – chronic leg pain, swelling, skin ulceration, reduced quality of life.
- **Common pitfalls**: 
  - Attributing swelling to “muscle strain” or “cellulitis” in young patients.
  - Missing isolated calf DVT (low Wells score, negative D-dimer if calf-only? actually D-dimer still positive, but ultrasound needed).
  - Starting anticoagulation without proper diagnosis (risk of bleeding in mimics like ruptured Baker’s cyst).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of how DVT develops:**  
It’s all about **Virchow’s triad** – stasis, hypercoagulability, endothelial injury.  
Imagine a postoperative hip arthroplasty patient:

1. **Endothelial injury**: Surgical trauma and bone reaming damage the vessel lining → exposes subendothelial tissue factor → initiates coagulation cascade.
2. **Stasis**: Immobilisation (bed rest, anaesthesia) slows blood flow in the deep calf sinuses and valves. Stasis prevents washout of activated clotting factors and allows clot to grow.
3. **Hypercoagulability**: The postoperative acute phase response increases fibrinogen and platelets; combined with any underlying thrombophilia (e.g., Factor V Leiden) creates a “storm” of procoagulant activity.

The thrombus typically starts in the **calf veins** (behind the medial malleolus) or in **valve pockets**. If left untreated, it propagates proximally into the popliteal, femoral, or iliac veins.

**Why each key sign happens:**

| Clinical sign | Pathophysiological mechanism |
|---------------|-----------------------------|
| Unilateral swelling | Venous obstruction raises hydrostatic pressure in capillaries → net filtration of fluid into tissue → oedema. |
| Calf pain | Stretching of the vein wall by distension + perivenous inflammation (pain afferents from adventitia) |
| Warmth | Inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, TNF) cause vasodilation → increased skin blood flow |
| Erythema | Same vasodilation + sometimes secondary superficial phlebitis |
| Dilated superficial collaterals | Over time, deep obstruction forces blood through superficial veins → visible “varicosities” |
| Positive Homan’s sign (dorsiflexion of foot causes calf pain) | Poor sensitivity/specificity – actually more from passive stretch of an inflamed gastrocnemius. **Do not rely on it.** |

**How this connects to the pattern recognition cues:**  
The postoperative patient with unilateral swelling, warmth, and pitting oedema = textbook stasis + injury. The asymmetry >3 cm is a strong clue because systemic causes (e.g., heart failure) would produce bilateral or more generalised oedema.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Specific cause | Stem clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Provoked (most common)** | Surgery (orthopaedic, pelvic, abdominal) | “Postoperative day 3–10, hip replacement” |
| | Immobilisation (long-haul flight, plaster cast, bed rest) | “Long flight >6 hours, leg in cast” |
| | Trauma (fracture, muscle injury) | “Fell on ice, leg swollen after tibial fracture” |
| | Pregnancy / puerperium | “3 weeks postpartum, unilateral leg swelling” |
| | Oestrogen therapy (OCP, HRT) | “Young woman on OCP, acute calf pain” |
| | Central venous catheter / pacemaker | “Subclavian line in situ, arm swelling” |
| **Active cancer** | Pancreatic, lung, gastric, haematologic, brain | “Unexplained DVT, recurrent, bilateral” |
| **Idiopathic (unprovoked)** | No obvious trigger after workup | “Healthy 55-year-old with no travel/surgery, no cancer on screen” |
| **Thrombophilia** | Factor V Leiden (resistance to activated protein C) | “Family history of venous thromboembolism, young onset” |
| | Prothrombin G20210A mutation | “Recurrent unprovoked DVT” |
| | Antiphospholipid syndrome | “Arterial + venous thrombosis, pregnancy loss, livedo reticularis” |
| | Antithrombin / protein C / protein S deficiency | “Neonatal purpura fulminans? Rare but suggestive” |
| **Inflammatory** | Behçet’s disease, IBD, vasculitis | “Oral/genital ulcers, uveitis, recurrent thrombosis” |

**Highlight:** The most common cause in hospital inpatients is **provoked** (recent surgery or immobilisation). In outpatients, **idiopathic/unprovoked** accounts for about 50% – always screen for occult malignancy.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
**Wells Score for DVT** – pre-test probability.  
- Score ≥2 = DVT likely (treat and ultrasound ASAP)  
- Score ≤1 = DVT unlikely (use D-dimer first)

| Clinical Feature | Points |
|------------------|--------|
| Active cancer (ongoing treatment, palliative, or diagnosed <6 months) | +1 |
| Paralysis, paresis, or recent plaster immobilisation of lower extremity | +1 |
| Recently bedridden >3 days, or major surgery within 12 weeks | +1 |
| Localised tenderness along deep venous system | +1 |
| Entire leg swollen | +1 |
| Calf swelling >3 cm compared with asymptomatic leg | +1 |
| Pitting oedema confined to symptomatic leg | +1 |
| Collateral superficial veins (non-varicose) | +1 |
| Previously documented DVT | +1 |
| Alternative diagnosis as likely or more likely (e.g., cellulitis, Baker's cyst) | −2 |

**Tests – what, why, how to interpret:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **D-dimer** (ELISA or rapid quantitative) | Fibrin degradation product – elevated when clot is being broken down | High sensitivity (~95%), low specificity (~40%). Used to **rule out** DVT when Wells score is unlikely. | **Negative D-dimer + low pre-test probability → DVT ruled out** (no need for ultrasound). Positive D-dimer → need imaging. |
| **Compression ultrasound (CUS)** of proximal veins | Real-time grey-scale image of vein lumen; compression applied | Gold standard for proximal DVT. **Non-compressibility** = clot. Also shows flow (doppler). | **Negative CUS (vein compresses fully) → DVT excluded in proximal segments.** If only calf symptoms, may need repeat scan in 7 days if suspicion remains high. |
| **Venography** (limited use) | Contrast dye outlines vein lumen | Reserved for equivocal cases, or when CUS not available (rare now) | Intraluminal filling defect = DVT. |

**Pattern recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out:**

- **Confirms**: Non-compressible deep vein on ultrasound at the popliteal or femoral level.
- **Rules out**: Negative D-dimer (low pre-test probability) OR fully compressible veins on ultrasound.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**

- **Anticoagulate** – unless contraindicated (active bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia, recent neuroaxial anaesthesia).
  - **LMWH** (e.g., enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC once daily, or 1 mg/kg SC twice daily) – provides rapid predictable anticoagulation, avoids monitoring (except anti-Xa in renal impairment/pregnancy).
  - **Fondaparinux** 5–10 mg SC daily (weight-based) if HIT history or alternative to LMWH.
  - **DOACs** (rivaroxaban 15 mg BD, apixaban 10 mg BD) can be used as first-line if no delay – same day start.
- **Why immediate?** Prevents clot extension and embolisation. The risk of PE is highest in the first 24 hours.
- **What NOT to do:** Do not delay anticoagulation for D-dimer or ultrasound if Wells score suggests likely DVT and no contraindications.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**

- **Transition to long-term agent:**
  - **DOACs** (preferred for most): rivaroxaban 15 mg PO BD for 21 days, then 20 mg OD; or apixaban 10 mg PO BD for 7 days, then 5 mg PO BD.
  - **Warfarin** if DOAC contraindicated (e.g., mechanical heart valve, antiphospholipid syndrome, severe liver impairment): start with LMWH overlap for ≥5 days until INR ≥2.0 for two consecutive days.
- **Mobilise** – early walking is safe (no evidence of increased PE risk), reduces swelling and post-thrombotic syndrome.
- **Compression stockings** – ankle-brachial index first; use 30–40 mmHg graduated stockings to reduce oedema and later post-thrombotic syndrome. Start within days if symptom relief needed.

**Long-term (duration):**

| Scenario | Duration | Rationale |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Provoked** (surgery, trauma, transient risk) | 3 months | Remove the trigger, clot stabilises. |
| **Unprovoked** (idiopathic) | At least 3–6 months; consider indefinite if high risk of recurrence (male, residual thrombus, elevated D-dimer after stopping) | Recurrence risk 10% per year after stopping; indefinite reduces that. |
| **Cancer-associated DVT** | At least 3–6 months, often indefinite (while cancer active) | LMWH or DOACs (rivaroxaban/edoxaban) preferred; recurrence high with warfarin. |
| **Recurrent unprovoked** | Indefinite anticoagulation | Very high recurrence risk. |

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

- **Don’t** stop anticoagulation early without assessing for thrombophilia (in unprovoked) – but wait until after acute event to test.
- **Don’t** massage the leg – could dislodge clot → iatrogenic PE.
- **Don’t** use aspirin alone for treatment – insufficient for VTE prevention/treatment.
- **Don’t** ignore renal function – dose adjust LMWH/DOACs in eGFR <30 mL/min.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | **DVT** | **Cellulitis** | **Ruptured Baker’s cyst** | **Superficial thrombophlebitis** |
|---------|---------|----------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Dull ache, worse with dependency, improves with elevation | Burning, spreading; skin painful to touch | Sudden sharp calf pain often after knee movement | Localised, tender cord along vein |
| **Exam** | Unilateral pitting oedema, warmth, erythema, dilated collaterals | Hot, red, well-demarcated erythema, systemic fever possible | Calf swelling, pitting, but often ecchymosis behind knee, knee joint effusion | Palpable, hardened, tender superficial vein (e.g., saphenous) |
| **ECG** | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | D-dimer ↑, CRP mild ↑ | WBC ↑, CRP ↑, D-dimer may be ↑ | D-dimer mild ↑, CRP mild ↑ | D-dimer may be ↑ |
| **Ultrasound** | Non-compressible deep vein | No clot in deep veins, thickened skin/soft tissue | Cystic structure in popliteal fossa, rupture shows fluid tracking | Non-compressible superficial vein (but not deep) |
| **Key differentiator** | Deep venous non-compressibility | Fever + skin demarcation + response to antibiotics | Cyst seen on ultrasound, knee effusion | Superficial vein involvement, no deep clot |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Pulmonary embolism (PE) – the most feared:**

- **Presentation**: sudden pleuritic chest pain, dyspnoea, haemoptysis, tachycardia, hypoxia.
- **Key signs**: ECG may show S1Q3T3 (acute cor pulmonale), right ventricular strain; CT pulmonary angiography confirms.
- **What to do**: Immediate high-flow oxygen, consider thrombolysis if massive PE with shock, or interventional/surgical embolectomy. Anticoagulate.
- **Late pattern**: “Missed DVT patient returns with cardiorespiratory arrest” – treat as suspected massive PE.

**Post-thrombotic syndrome (40–60% of patients at 2 years):**

- **Presentation**: chronic leg pain, swelling, heaviness, skin changes (lipodermatosclerosis, pigmentation), venous ulcers.
- **Mechanism**: venous valvular incompetence + persistent obstruction → chronic venous hypertension.
- **Prevention**: early mobilization, compression stockings (30–40 mmHg for 2 years), adequate anticoagulation duration.
- **What to do** if develops: lifelong compression, wound care, consider venoplasty or stenting for iliac vein obstruction.

**Phlegmasia cerulea dolens (rare but limb-threatening):**

- **Massive iliofemoral DVT** that completely obstructs venous outflow → arterial compromise.
- **Presentation**: sudden, severe leg pain; blue-purple discolouration (cyanosis); cold, weak, pulseless limb; oedema massive.
- **What to do**: **emergency** – call vascular surgery; consider catheter-directed thrombolysis or surgical thrombectomy. Anticoagulate urgently. Risk of compartment syndrome (need fasciotomy) and venous gangrene.

**Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)** – rare but late complication of undiagnosed/recurrent PE.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **THROMBOSIS** (risk factors):  
  T – Travel (prolonged)  
  H – Hormones (OCP, HRT)  
  R – Recent surgery/trauma  
  O – Obesity  
  M – Malignancy  
  B – Behçet’s / IBD  
  O – Obstetrics (pregnancy/puerperium)  
  S – Stasis (immobilisation)  
  I – Inherited thrombophilia  
  S – Smoking (moderate)

- **Wells DVT points recall:** active **C**ancer, **C**ast/paralysis, **B**edridden >3d, **T**enderness, **S**welling entire leg, **C**alf >3cm, **P**itting oedema, **C**ollaterals, **P**revious DVT, minus 2 if **A**lternative diagnosis. Mnemonic: *Caught By Tender Swollen Calves, Pitting, Collaterals, Previous, minus Alternative.*

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Always measure both calves** at a fixed distance (e.g., 10 cm below tibial tuberosity). A difference >3 cm is a strong clue, but even 1 cm warrants consideration.
2. **D-dimer is very sensitive but non-specific** – only trust a negative result to rule out DVT if pre-test probability is low. Postoperative patients have positive D-dimer anyway, so skip D-dimer and go straight to ultrasound.
3. **If the patient has cancer, do not use D-dimer to rule out** – order ultrasound directly because sensitivity drops.
4. **Calf DVT** – do not treat all calf DVTs the same: isolated distal DVT (below popliteal) with low risk may be monitored with serial ultrasound after 2 weeks, but most centres anticoagulate for 6 weeks to prevent propagation.
5. **Homan’s sign is unreliable** – do not let a negative Homan’s make you miss DVT.
6. **Upper limb DVT** – always check for central line, pacemaker, or thoracic outlet syndrome; anticoagulate same as leg DVT.
7. **Pregnancy** – D-dimer norms change with gestational age; use diagnostic ultrasound as first line; LMWH is safe (warfarin teratogenic).

**Common ward/ exam mistakes to avoid:**

- Ordering D-dimer in a patient with high pre-test probability (should go straight to ultrasound)
- Starting warfarin without bridging LMWH (risk of paradoxical thrombosis from protein C deficiency)
- Forgetting to check renal function before dosing enoxaparin
- Stopping anticoagulation after 3 months in unprovoked DVT without discussing risks of recurrence
- Not assessing for malignancy in unprovoked DVT (CT chest-abdomen-pelvis, basic bloods)
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call a senior or the crash team:**

| Clinical scenario | What to do |
|------------------|------------|
| **Suspected massive PE** (hypotension, shock, severe hypoxia) | **Call crash team / cardiac arrest call.** Immediate high-flow O₂, fluid resuscitation, consider thrombolysis. |
| **Phlegmasia cerulea dolens** (leg blue, cold, pulseless) | **Call vascular surgery on-call urgently.** Start IV heparin/MH, prepare for possible thrombolysis or thrombectomy. |
| **DVT with active bleeding** (e.g., GI bleed, intracranial haemorrhage) | **Call senior and haematology.** Stop anticoagulation, reverse if needed (protamine for LMWH, PCC for DOACs), urgent CT as indicated. |
| **Suspected heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)** (platelet drop >50% or new thrombosis on LMWH/heparin) | **Call haematology.** Stop all heparin, start alternative (fondaparinux, argatroban). Send HIT antibody test. |
| **NEWS2 ≥5 or rising** (tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypoxia, hypotension) | Escalate to medical registrar or critical care outreach. Consider PE vs. sepsis. |
| **Lactate >2 mmol/L** with signs of shock | Sign of tissue hypoperfusion – may indicate massive PE or severe sepsis – escalate to ICU. |
| **GCS <15** and sudden onset | Could be from hypoxic brain injury due to PE – urgent call for airway support. |

**Always communicate with senior: “I have a patient with [clinical suspicion of DVT/PE] who

rawMarkdown:
## Deep Vein Thrombosis
**One-liner:** A blood clot in a deep vein (usually leg), driven by stasis, hypercoagulability, or endothelial injury; it matters because it can embolise to cause pulmonary embolism and chronic venous damage.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 62-year-old man, 4 days post elective total hip replacement, develops unilateral right calf pain and swelling. The leg feels warm, the skin is mildly erythematous, and there is pitting oedema from the mid-calf down. He has been mobilising less than usual and has a history of hypertension but no prior clotting disorders. His calf circumference is 3 cm greater on the right than the left.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Postoperative (hip replacement) | **Provoked DVT**: immobility + tissue trauma = endothelial injury + stasis |
| Unilateral leg swelling | **Venous obstruction**: clot blocks outflow, increased hydrostatic pressure pushes fluid out |
| Warmth and erythema | **Local inflammatory response**: perivenous inflammation releases cytokines, dilates vessels |
| Pitting oedema | **Interstitial fluid accumulation** due to raised venous pressure |
| Asymmetric calf circumference (>3 cm) | **Highly suggestive of DVT**; even a 1–2 cm difference deserves investigation |
| Decreased mobility | **Venous stasis** → clot propagation risk |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Isolated calf DVT**: mild pain or no swelling, normal calf circumference; often missed until it propagates proximally.
- **Bilateral DVT**: rare; think malignancy (causes hypercoagulable state) or inferior vena cava filter/obstruction.
- **Upper extremity DVT** (effort thrombosis / Paget-Schroetter syndrome): arm swelling, cyanosis in a young athlete after strenuous use; also consider central line or pacemaker lead.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Definition:** Deep vein thrombosis is the formation of a blood clot (thrombus) within a deep vein, most commonly in the leg (proximal – popliteal, femoral, iliac; or distal – calf veins). The clot obstructs venous return and triggers a local inflammatory response.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency**: 1–2 per 1000 adults annually; much higher in hospitalized, surgical, or cancer patients.
- **Stakes**: 
  - Pulmonary embolism (PE) – sudden death in severe cases.
  - Post-thrombotic syndrome – chronic leg pain, swelling, skin ulceration, reduced quality of life.
- **Common pitfalls**: 
  - Attributing swelling to “muscle strain” or “cellulitis” in young patients.
  - Missing isolated calf DVT (low Wells score, negative D-dimer if calf-only? actually D-dimer still positive, but ultrasound needed).
  - Starting anticoagulation without proper diagnosis (risk of bleeding in mimics like ruptured Baker’s cyst).

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of how DVT develops:**  
It’s all about **Virchow’s triad** – stasis, hypercoagulability, endothelial injury.  
Imagine a postoperative hip arthroplasty patient:

1. **Endothelial injury**: Surgical trauma and bone reaming damage the vessel lining → exposes subendothelial tissue factor → initiates coagulation cascade.
2. **Stasis**: Immobilisation (bed rest, anaesthesia) slows blood flow in the deep calf sinuses and valves. Stasis prevents washout of activated clotting factors and allows clot to grow.
3. **Hypercoagulability**: The postoperative acute phase response increases fibrinogen and platelets; combined with any underlying thrombophilia (e.g., Factor V Leiden) creates a “storm” of procoagulant activity.

The thrombus typically starts in the **calf veins** (behind the medial malleolus) or in **valve pockets**. If left untreated, it propagates proximally into the popliteal, femoral, or iliac veins.

**Why each key sign happens:**

| Clinical sign | Pathophysiological mechanism |
|---------------|-----------------------------|
| Unilateral swelling | Venous obstruction raises hydrostatic pressure in capillaries → net filtration of fluid into tissue → oedema. |
| Calf pain | Stretching of the vein wall by distension + perivenous inflammation (pain afferents from adventitia) |
| Warmth | Inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, TNF) cause vasodilation → increased skin blood flow |
| Erythema | Same vasodilation + sometimes secondary superficial phlebitis |
| Dilated superficial collaterals | Over time, deep obstruction forces blood through superficial veins → visible “varicosities” |
| Positive Homan’s sign (dorsiflexion of foot causes calf pain) | Poor sensitivity/specificity – actually more from passive stretch of an inflamed gastrocnemius. **Do not rely on it.** |

**How this connects to the pattern recognition cues:**  
The postoperative patient with unilateral swelling, warmth, and pitting oedema = textbook stasis + injury. The asymmetry >3 cm is a strong clue because systemic causes (e.g., heart failure) would produce bilateral or more generalised oedema.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Specific cause | Stem clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Provoked (most common)** | Surgery (orthopaedic, pelvic, abdominal) | “Postoperative day 3–10, hip replacement” |
| | Immobilisation (long-haul flight, plaster cast, bed rest) | “Long flight >6 hours, leg in cast” |
| | Trauma (fracture, muscle injury) | “Fell on ice, leg swollen after tibial fracture” |
| | Pregnancy / puerperium | “3 weeks postpartum, unilateral leg swelling” |
| | Oestrogen therapy (OCP, HRT) | “Young woman on OCP, acute calf pain” |
| | Central venous catheter / pacemaker | “Subclavian line in situ, arm swelling” |
| **Active cancer** | Pancreatic, lung, gastric, haematologic, brain | “Unexplained DVT, recurrent, bilateral” |
| **Idiopathic (unprovoked)** | No obvious trigger after workup | “Healthy 55-year-old with no travel/surgery, no cancer on screen” |
| **Thrombophilia** | Factor V Leiden (resistance to activated protein C) | “Family history of venous thromboembolism, young onset” |
| | Prothrombin G20210A mutation | “Recurrent unprovoked DVT” |
| | Antiphospholipid syndrome | “Arterial + venous thrombosis, pregnancy loss, livedo reticularis” |
| | Antithrombin / protein C / protein S deficiency | “Neonatal purpura fulminans? Rare but suggestive” |
| **Inflammatory** | Behçet’s disease, IBD, vasculitis | “Oral/genital ulcers, uveitis, recurrent thrombosis” |

**Highlight:** The most common cause in hospital inpatients is **provoked** (recent surgery or immobilisation). In outpatients, **idiopathic/unprovoked** accounts for about 50% – always screen for occult malignancy.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
**Wells Score for DVT** – pre-test probability.  
- Score ≥2 = DVT likely (treat and ultrasound ASAP)  
- Score ≤1 = DVT unlikely (use D-dimer first)

| Clinical Feature | Points |
|------------------|--------|
| Active cancer (ongoing treatment, palliative, or diagnosed <6 months) | +1 |
| Paralysis, paresis, or recent plaster immobilisation of lower extremity | +1 |
| Recently bedridden >3 days, or major surgery within 12 weeks | +1 |
| Localised tenderness along deep venous system | +1 |
| Entire leg swollen | +1 |
| Calf swelling >3 cm compared with asymptomatic leg | +1 |
| Pitting oedema confined to symptomatic leg | +1 |
| Collateral superficial veins (non-varicose) | +1 |
| Previously documented DVT | +1 |
| Alternative diagnosis as likely or more likely (e.g., cellulitis, Baker's cyst) | −2 |

**Tests – what, why, how to interpret:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **D-dimer** (ELISA or rapid quantitative) | Fibrin degradation product – elevated when clot is being broken down | High sensitivity (~95%), low specificity (~40%). Used to **rule out** DVT when Wells score is unlikely. | **Negative D-dimer + low pre-test probability → DVT ruled out** (no need for ultrasound). Positive D-dimer → need imaging. |
| **Compression ultrasound (CUS)** of proximal veins | Real-time grey-scale image of vein lumen; compression applied | Gold standard for proximal DVT. **Non-compressibility** = clot. Also shows flow (doppler). | **Negative CUS (vein compresses fully) → DVT excluded in proximal segments.** If only calf symptoms, may need repeat scan in 7 days if suspicion remains high. |
| **Venography** (limited use) | Contrast dye outlines vein lumen | Reserved for equivocal cases, or when CUS not available (rare now) | Intraluminal filling defect = DVT. |

**Pattern recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out:**

- **Confirms**: Non-compressible deep vein on ultrasound at the popliteal or femoral level.
- **Rules out**: Negative D-dimer (low pre-test probability) OR fully compressible veins on ultrasound.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**

- **Anticoagulate** – unless contraindicated (active bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia, recent neuroaxial anaesthesia).
  - **LMWH** (e.g., enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC once daily, or 1 mg/kg SC twice daily) – provides rapid predictable anticoagulation, avoids monitoring (except anti-Xa in renal impairment/pregnancy).
  - **Fondaparinux** 5–10 mg SC daily (weight-based) if HIT history or alternative to LMWH.
  - **DOACs** (rivaroxaban 15 mg BD, apixaban 10 mg BD) can be used as first-line if no delay – same day start.
- **Why immediate?** Prevents clot extension and embolisation. The risk of PE is highest in the first 24 hours.
- **What NOT to do:** Do not delay anticoagulation for D-dimer or ultrasound if Wells score suggests likely DVT and no contraindications.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**

- **Transition to long-term agent:**
  - **DOACs** (preferred for most): rivaroxaban 15 mg PO BD for 21 days, then 20 mg OD; or apixaban 10 mg PO BD for 7 days, then 5 mg PO BD.
  - **Warfarin** if DOAC contraindicated (e.g., mechanical heart valve, antiphospholipid syndrome, severe liver impairment): start with LMWH overlap for ≥5 days until INR ≥2.0 for two consecutive days.
- **Mobilise** – early walking is safe (no evidence of increased PE risk), reduces swelling and post-thrombotic syndrome.
- **Compression stockings** – ankle-brachial index first; use 30–40 mmHg graduated stockings to reduce oedema and later post-thrombotic syndrome. Start within days if symptom relief needed.

**Long-term (duration):**

| Scenario | Duration | Rationale |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Provoked** (surgery, trauma, transient risk) | 3 months | Remove the trigger, clot stabilises. |
| **Unprovoked** (idiopathic) | At least 3–6 months; consider indefinite if high risk of recurrence (male, residual thrombus, elevated D-dimer after stopping) | Recurrence risk 10% per year after stopping; indefinite reduces that. |
| **Cancer-associated DVT** | At least 3–6 months, often indefinite (while cancer active) | LMWH or DOACs (rivaroxaban/edoxaban) preferred; recurrence high with warfarin. |
| **Recurrent unprovoked** | Indefinite anticoagulation | Very high recurrence risk. |

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

- **Don’t** stop anticoagulation early without assessing for thrombophilia (in unprovoked) – but wait until after acute event to test.
- **Don’t** massage the leg – could dislodge clot → iatrogenic PE.
- **Don’t** use aspirin alone for treatment – insufficient for VTE prevention/treatment.
- **Don’t** ignore renal function – dose adjust LMWH/DOACs in eGFR <30 mL/min.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | **DVT** | **Cellulitis** | **Ruptured Baker’s cyst** | **Superficial thrombophlebitis** |
|---------|---------|----------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Dull ache, worse with dependency, improves with elevation | Burning, spreading; skin painful to touch | Sudden sharp calf pain often after knee movement | Localised, tender cord along vein |
| **Exam** | Unilateral pitting oedema, warmth, erythema, dilated collaterals | Hot, red, well-demarcated erythema, systemic fever possible | Calf swelling, pitting, but often ecchymosis behind knee, knee joint effusion | Palpable, hardened, tender superficial vein (e.g., saphenous) |
| **ECG** | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | D-dimer ↑, CRP mild ↑ | WBC ↑, CRP ↑, D-dimer may be ↑ | D-dimer mild ↑, CRP mild ↑ | D-dimer may be ↑ |
| **Ultrasound** | Non-compressible deep vein | No clot in deep veins, thickened skin/soft tissue | Cystic structure in popliteal fossa, rupture shows fluid tracking | Non-compressible superficial vein (but not deep) |
| **Key differentiator** | Deep venous non-compressibility | Fever + skin demarcation + response to antibiotics | Cyst seen on ultrasound, knee effusion | Superficial vein involvement, no deep clot |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Pulmonary embolism (PE) – the most feared:**

- **Presentation**: sudden pleuritic chest pain, dyspnoea, haemoptysis, tachycardia, hypoxia.
- **Key signs**: ECG may show S1Q3T3 (acute cor pulmonale), right ventricular strain; CT pulmonary angiography confirms.
- **What to do**: Immediate high-flow oxygen, consider thrombolysis if massive PE with shock, or interventional/surgical embolectomy. Anticoagulate.
- **Late pattern**: “Missed DVT patient returns with cardiorespiratory arrest” – treat as suspected massive PE.

**Post-thrombotic syndrome (40–60% of patients at 2 years):**

- **Presentation**: chronic leg pain, swelling, heaviness, skin changes (lipodermatosclerosis, pigmentation), venous ulcers.
- **Mechanism**: venous valvular incompetence + persistent obstruction → chronic venous hypertension.
- **Prevention**: early mobilization, compression stockings (30–40 mmHg for 2 years), adequate anticoagulation duration.
- **What to do** if develops: lifelong compression, wound care, consider venoplasty or stenting for iliac vein obstruction.

**Phlegmasia cerulea dolens (rare but limb-threatening):**

- **Massive iliofemoral DVT** that completely obstructs venous outflow → arterial compromise.
- **Presentation**: sudden, severe leg pain; blue-purple discolouration (cyanosis); cold, weak, pulseless limb; oedema massive.
- **What to do**: **emergency** – call vascular surgery; consider catheter-directed thrombolysis or surgical thrombectomy. Anticoagulate urgently. Risk of compartment syndrome (need fasciotomy) and venous gangrene.

**Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)** – rare but late complication of undiagnosed/recurrent PE.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **THROMBOSIS** (risk factors):  
  T – Travel (prolonged)  
  H – Hormones (OCP, HRT)  
  R – Recent surgery/trauma  
  O – Obesity  
  M – Malignancy  
  B – Behçet’s / IBD  
  O – Obstetrics (pregnancy/puerperium)  
  S – Stasis (immobilisation)  
  I – Inherited thrombophilia  
  S – Smoking (moderate)

- **Wells DVT points recall:** active **C**ancer, **C**ast/paralysis, **B**edridden >3d, **T**enderness, **S**welling entire leg, **C**alf >3cm, **P**itting oedema, **C**ollaterals, **P**revious DVT, minus 2 if **A**lternative diagnosis. Mnemonic: *Caught By Tender Swollen Calves, Pitting, Collaterals, Previous, minus Alternative.*

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Always measure both calves** at a fixed distance (e.g., 10 cm below tibial tuberosity). A difference >3 cm is a strong clue, but even 1 cm warrants consideration.
2. **D-dimer is very sensitive but non-specific** – only trust a negative result to rule out DVT if pre-test probability is low. Postoperative patients have positive D-dimer anyway, so skip D-dimer and go straight to ultrasound.
3. **If the patient has cancer, do not use D-dimer to rule out** – order ultrasound directly because sensitivity drops.
4. **Calf DVT** – do not treat all calf DVTs the same: isolated distal DVT (below popliteal) with low risk may be monitored with serial ultrasound after 2 weeks, but most centres anticoagulate for 6 weeks to prevent propagation.
5. **Homan’s sign is unreliable** – do not let a negative Homan’s make you miss DVT.
6. **Upper limb DVT** – always check for central line, pacemaker, or thoracic outlet syndrome; anticoagulate same as leg DVT.
7. **Pregnancy** – D-dimer norms change with gestational age; use diagnostic ultrasound as first line; LMWH is safe (warfarin teratogenic).

**Common ward/ exam mistakes to avoid:**

- Ordering D-dimer in a patient with high pre-test probability (should go straight to ultrasound)
- Starting warfarin without bridging LMWH (risk of paradoxical thrombosis from protein C deficiency)
- Forgetting to check renal function before dosing enoxaparin
- Stopping anticoagulation after 3 months in unprovoked DVT without discussing risks of recurrence
- Not assessing for malignancy in unprovoked DVT (CT chest-abdomen-pelvis, basic bloods)

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call a senior or the crash team:**

| Clinical scenario | What to do |
|------------------|------------|
| **Suspected massive PE** (hypotension, shock, severe hypoxia) | **Call crash team / cardiac arrest call.** Immediate high-flow O₂, fluid resuscitation, consider thrombolysis. |
| **Phlegmasia cerulea dolens** (leg blue, cold, pulseless) | **Call vascular surgery on-call urgently.** Start IV heparin/MH, prepare for possible thrombolysis or thrombectomy. |
| **DVT with active bleeding** (e.g., GI bleed, intracranial haemorrhage) | **Call senior and haematology.** Stop anticoagulation, reverse if needed (protamine for LMWH, PCC for DOACs), urgent CT as indicated. |
| **Suspected heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)** (platelet drop >50% or new thrombosis on LMWH/heparin) | **Call haematology.** Stop all heparin, start alternative (fondaparinux, argatroban). Send HIT antibody test. |
| **NEWS2 ≥5 or rising** (tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypoxia, hypotension) | Escalate to medical registrar or critical care outreach. Consider PE vs. sepsis. |
| **Lactate >2 mmol/L** with signs of shock | Sign of tissue hypoperfusion – may indicate massive PE or severe sepsis – escalate to ICU. |
| **GCS <15** and sudden onset | Could be from hypoxic brain injury due to PE – urgent call for airway support. |

**Always communicate with senior: “I have a patient with [clinical suspicion of DVT/PE] who
